    Cosmic Dawn

    Cosmic Dawn

    Type:Cosmological epoch
    Level:Specialist
    Redshift:z ≈ 10–20 (cosmic age ~150–500 Myr)
    Defining process:Formation of first galaxies, onset of reionization
    Related: High-redshift galaxies
    Red monsters
    Blue monsters
    Star formation
    Interstellar dust

    Cosmic dawn refers to the epoch at z ≈ 10–20 (cosmic ages ~150–500 Myr after the Big Bang) when the first galaxies assembled, produced the first heavy elements and dust, and began ionizing the neutral intergalactic medium. This period marks the transition from a dark, neutral universe to one illuminated by starlight and structured into the first gravitationally bound systems. JWST has opened a direct observational window into this epoch, revealing a surprisingly rich population of luminous galaxies[1][2].

    Physical Conditions

    At cosmic dawn, the Universe is less than ~500 Myr old. The available time for stellar mass assembly, chemical enrichment, and dust production is severely limited. Standard cosmological parameters[3] give the age of the Universe as ~400 Myr at z ~ 11.5 and ~300 Myr at z ~ 14. Despite these short timescales, JWST observations reveal galaxies with stellar masses up to log(M*/M⊙) ~ 9–10 and significant dust content, implying extremely efficient early assembly[4][5].

    Galaxy Assembly and Feedback

    Several theoretical frameworks compete to explain the rapid buildup of galaxies at cosmic dawn:

    • Radiative-feedback model (Ferrara+2024): Galaxies undergo alternating “red monster” (dust-obscured, rapid assembly) and “blue monster” (UV-bright, dust-free) phases. During the red phase (~20% of lifetime), ~70% of stellar mass forms in a dust-enshrouded environment before radiation-driven outflows clear the dust[6].
    • Feedback-free model (Dekel+2023): Early galaxies grow rapidly via cold gas inflows without significant feedback regulation, explaining high UV luminosities and stellar masses[7].
    • Density-regulated SF (Somerville+2025): Star formation efficiency depends on local gas density, with semi-analytic models reproducing observed galaxy properties[8].

    Dust and Metals at z > 10

    The discovery of dust-reddened galaxies at cosmic dawn challenges models predicting slow grain growth through ISM accretion[9]. EGS-z11-R0 at z = 11.45 exhibits AV ~ 1 mag and detection of [Fe V] λ4227, providing direct evidence that iron-peak elements — key constituents of dust grains — were already present in the ISM at this epoch[5]. Iron enrichment at z > 11 is consistent with prompt release from massive stars within ~10 Myr of formation[10]. These observations imply that dust production through stellar sources (supernova ejecta, AGB star winds) and rapid grain growth in dense environments may dominate over slow ISM accretion at the earliest times.

    Ionizing Sources and AGN

    Emission-line diagnostics at cosmic dawn reveal hard ionizing radiation fields, but separating AGN from extreme stellar ionization remains challenging. UV diagnostic diagrams (C IV/C III] vs. C III]/He II ratios) consistently place z > 10 galaxies like GN-z11[11], GHZ9[12], and EGS-z11-R0[5] in composite regions. The possibility of early black-hole growth through massive seed channels (direct-collapse black holes, primordial black holes) is consistent with the inferred AGN contributions and black-hole masses[5][13].

    See Also

    • High-redshift galaxies — the broader population
    • Red monsters — dust-obscured galaxies at cosmic dawn
    • Blue monsters — UV-bright galaxies after dust clearing
    • Grain growth — dust production mechanism challenged at z > 10
    • Rodighiero et al. (2026) — EGS-z11-R0 at z = 11.45

    References

    1. Naidu, R. P. et al. (2022). "Two remarkably luminous galaxy candidates at z ≈ 10–12." ApJ, 940, L14.
    2. Harikane, Y. et al. (2022). "A search for H-dropout Lyman Break Galaxies at z ~ 12–16." ApJ, 929, 1.
    3. Planck Collaboration (2020). "Planck 2018 results. VI. Cosmological parameters." A&A, 641, A6.
    4. Santini, P. et al. (2025). "High stellar masses and sSFR at z > 10 indicating rapid assembly."
    5. Rodighiero, G. et al. (2026). "EGS-z11-R0: a red, dust-rich galaxy at Cosmic Dawn." arXiv:2603.15841
    6. Ferrara, A. (2024). "Red Monsters and Blue Monsters: radiative feedback and the early growth of galaxies."
    7. Dekel, A. et al. (2023). "Feedback-free model of early galaxy growth via cold gas inflows."
    8. Somerville, R. S. et al. (2025). "Semi-analytic models with density-regulated star formation."
    9. Narayanan, D. et al. (2025). "Cosmological simulations with dust evolution and radiative transfer."
    10. Nakane, M. et al. (2025). "Stellar iron abundances at z = 9–12 from NIRSpec spectra."
    11. Bunker, A. J. et al. (2023). "JADES NIRSpec spectroscopy of GN-z11." A&A, 677, A88.
    12. Napolitano, L. et al. (2025). "GHZ9: composite AGN+stellar analysis at z = 10.145."
    13. Matteri, L. et al. "Primordial black hole scenarios contributing to UV luminosity of super-early galaxies."
